verdict_reasoning: C3 is a serum complement component synthesized primarily by hepatocytes and secreted into plasma. The mature protein (C3α/C3β dimer) circulates in blood as a soluble protein. Complement activation generates soluble peptide products (C3a anaphylatoxin, C3b, iC3b) via proteolysis in solution or bound transiently to pathogen/cell surfaces during immune responses. C3 itself lacks a transmembrane domain, GPI anchor, or documented outer-leaflet lipidation. While C3b can bind covalently to target surfaces during complement cascade amplification, the C3 protein body is not stably anchored to the plasma membrane of living cells—it is a soluble protein in equilibrium with the plasma/extracellular fluid. The covalent C3b deposition products are fragments resulting from proteolytic cleavage in the extracellular space, not stable anchoring of the intact C3 protein body to a cell-surface TM partner.
